open_questions: When the goal says "the agent," which concrete thing should be made faster? Candidates in this repo: (a) the Pactum CLI orchestrator itself (Go code in cmd/pactum + internal/app: map scan, search index, prompt build, gate); (b) a specific agent role Pactum runs — executor / reviewer / clarifier / drafter / fixer (internal/agents.AgentDescriptor); (c) the external agent CLIs codex/claude that Pactum invokes (docs/agents.md states Pactum does NOT install, configure, or wrap these, so their speed is largely outside Pactum's control); or (d) the end-to-end run pipeline as the operator experiences it.

What does "faster" measure? Candidates: (a) wall-clock duration of a run/stage (RunResult.DurationMillis); (b) token usage / cost as a latency-and-spend proxy (TokenUsage, docs/cost-budget-design.md); (c) fewer agent round-trips / subprocess invocations (e.g. fewer review-loop rounds); or (d) the Pactum Go process's own overhead independent of any agent call.

How will 'faster' be verified as done? The contract has no acceptance_criteria and no validation commands, so 'faster' is currently unverifiable. What baseline, target, and measurement method define completion — e.g. a named representative scenario, a captured baseline, a concrete target (absolute or percentage reduction), and a repeatable command that measures it?

Is changing observable behavior or output acceptable as a trade-off for speed, or must speed improve while preserving existing behavior and correctness? For example: may a stage be dropped, review rounds reduced (limits.review.*), or context/prompt tokens trimmed if that changes what the agent produces — or must all existing tests and outputs stay unchanged?

questions.question: When the goal says "the agent," which concrete thing should be made faster? Candidates in this repo: (a) the Pactum CLI orchestrator itself (Go code in cmd/pactum + internal/app: map scan, search index, prompt build, gate); (b) a specific agent role Pactum runs — executor / reviewer / clarifier / drafter / fixer (internal/agents.AgentDescriptor); (c) the external agent CLIs codex/claude that Pactum invokes (docs/agents.md states Pactum does NOT install, configure, or wrap these, so their speed is largely outside Pactum's control); or (d) the end-to-end run pipeline as the operator experiences it.
questions.rationale: Pactum has no single 'the agent.' docs/agents.md and docs/flow.md show an orchestrator that runs multiple agent ROLES against external CLIs. Each candidate points at a completely different change surface, and option (c) is mostly out of Pactum's control by design. The contract's empty scope means nothing in the repo disambiguates this — it is the human's intent to settle. Every later answer depends on it.
questions.recommended_answer: Set scope to the Pactum-orchestrated run as a whole (d), but constrained to what Pactum actually controls: its own Go execution (map/scan/search/prompt-build/gate) and the number and size of agent turns it issues. Explicitly exclude (c) — do not attempt to speed up codex/claude themselves or change their models. Add the chosen target to the contract goal, e.g. 'Reduce the wall-clock time of a Pactum run, limited to overhead Pactum controls (not the external agent CLIs' own latency).'

questions.question: What does "faster" measure? Candidates: (a) wall-clock duration of a run/stage (RunResult.DurationMillis); (b) token usage / cost as a latency-and-spend proxy (TokenUsage, docs/cost-budget-design.md); (c) fewer agent round-trips / subprocess invocations (e.g. fewer review-loop rounds); or (d) the Pactum Go process's own overhead independent of any agent call.
questions.rationale: 'Faster' is overloaded across at least four measurable dimensions that the repo already instruments separately (DurationMillis vs TokenUsage vs loop rounds). Optimizing one can be neutral or harmful to another (e.g. more caching tokens to cut latency). Which dimension is primary determines what code to touch and how to verify, and the empty acceptance section gives no signal.
questions.recommended_answer: Define 'faster' as primary = wall-clock duration of a representative run (RunResult.DurationMillis), with token usage (TokenUsage) recorded as a secondary, must-not-regress metric. Record both as assumptions in the contract.

questions.question: How will 'faster' be verified as done? The contract has no acceptance_criteria and no validation commands, so 'faster' is currently unverifiable. What baseline, target, and measurement method define completion — e.g. a named representative scenario, a captured baseline, a concrete target (absolute or percentage reduction), and a repeatable command that measures it?
questions.rationale: docs/flow.md shows acceptance criteria and validation commands are the gate for completion, and both are empty here. Without a baseline + target + measurement, the executor cannot know when it is done and the gate cannot check it. This is a decision the repo cannot make: it needs a chosen scenario and target number.
questions.recommended_answer: Add an acceptance criterion: 'On the chosen representative scenario, median wall-clock time over 3 runs is reduced ≥20% versus the recorded pre-change baseline, with no token-usage regression and all existing tests passing.' Add validation command(s): the existing suite `go test ./...` (correctness guard) plus a documented timing command for the chosen scenario; record the measured baseline as an assumption.

questions.question: Is changing observable behavior or output acceptable as a trade-off for speed, or must speed improve while preserving existing behavior and correctness? For example: may a stage be dropped, review rounds reduced (limits.review.*), or context/prompt tokens trimmed if that changes what the agent produces — or must all existing tests and outputs stay unchanged?
questions.rationale: Several plausible speedups (fewer review-loop rounds, smaller prompt context, skipping a stage) trade correctness/coverage for speed. With empty scope, an executor could legitimately regress behavior to hit a speed target. Whether such trade-offs are allowed is a product call the repo cannot infer.
questions.recommended_answer: Add to scope.out: 'No change to observable behavior, command outputs, or contract/gate semantics; all existing tests (go test ./...) must continue to pass.' Add to scope.in: 'Speed gains limited to internal/deterministic optimizations and reductions in redundant work that do not alter outputs.' If behavior-changing trade-offs (e.g. fewer review rounds) are intended, they must be listed explicitly instead.
